text: The surgeon uses hand controls and foot pedals at the master console to manipulate four robotic instruments inside the patient's body.
The robotic surgical system is switched on, integrating itself through visible fiber optic cables as the button is pressed.
The system performs self-checks on the patient card and surgeon console, with all components visibly integrated using blue fiber optic cables.

The video shows the surgeon console with hand controls being tested for movement. The patient cart arms are also checked for proper function. The touch screen on the console displays a message indicating the system is ready.
The touch screen displays a message to deploy the bool, showcasing its telestration facility.
The assistant uses the telestration feature on the touch screen to point out structures, which are communicated to the surgeon console, facilitating interaction between the assistant and the surgeon.

The video shows the diaphragm and the video processor unit where the camera will be attached. The patient cart is visible with four universal arms, contrasting with the previous SI system that had one camera arm and three instrument arms.
The video shows the da Vinci surgical system with four universal arms, each capable of accommodating the camera for surgical procedures.
The video shows the instrument arms of the surgical system, which are controlled by buttons labeled as the instrument clutch.
Pressing the instrument clutch causes the LED to blink blue, indicating that the instrument or arm can be moved in any direction, although it does not control the proximal set of joints.
The patient clearance button is pressed, causing the robotic arm to rotate its joints to prevent external arm clashing.
The boom rotation button is pressed, causing the entire boom to rotate. A green targeting light is visible, pointing down towards the endoscope area.
The green cross is visible, pointing towards the endoscope area, indicating the target location for docking.
The touch screen displays controls for the movement of the patient cart, including a throttle enable switch that, when pressed, allows the cart to move forward or backward.
The controls are used to adjust the boom position and height, followed by draping and selecting the allotomy setting on the surgical system.
The surgeon uses handles to adjust the position of the patient cart, aligning it precisely as required for the surgical procedure.

The DaVinci XI surgical console is shown, where the surgeon sits to control the instruments. The console features a stereo viewer that provides a three-dimensional high-definition view of the surgical field.
The surgical console features hand controls that the surgeon uses to manipulate instruments with both hands, along with foot pedals for additional control.
The surgical console features foot pedals including diathermy pedals for the right instrument, energy pedals for the left instrument, a camera pedal, a clutch pedal, and a swap pedal.
The swap paddle on the Da Vinci XI surgical console is shown, used for switching between various instruments. A brake mechanism is visible, allowing the master console to remain stationary.
The ergonomic controls of the DaVinci XI master console are shown, demonstrating adjustable features such as height and tilt for user comfort, including the hand rest height adjustment.

The infrared sensors detect when the surgeon's head is positioned inside the stereo viewer, activating the instruments. When the head is removed, the sensors deactivate the instruments, preventing movement when the surgeon is not viewing the surgical field.

The video shows the finger clutches on the surgical console, which allow the master controllers to move without affecting the position of the instrument tips. The surgeon demonstrates pressing a button on the finger clutches to enable this movement.
The robotic endoscope is visible, marked with 'zero degree,' featuring two endoscopes integrated into one unit.
The video shows the tip of an 8mm endoscope with two integrated scopes and four light sources, providing bright illumination. The endoscope is currently set to a zero-degree angle.

The robotic system features a plate for attachment to the patient cart, with buttons to control the illuminator. Pressing the button for a few seconds switches on the illuminator. Another button allows for taking intraoperative pictures if a USB drive is connected to the vision cart.
The video shows the targeting button used for docking the patient cart, alongside the robotic instruments that will be utilized during the procedure.

The video shows white-colored robotic instruments designed for human use and red-colored instruments intended for training purposes. The instruments feature a housing with plates containing discs or pulleys that translate the surgeon's hand movements into precise instrument tip movements.

The tip of the robotic instrument demonstrates seven degrees of freedom, allowing it to move up, right, rotate, and open and close. Fine wires and pulleys at the tip control these movements, visible in the force bipolar instrument.

The false bipolar instrument is shown with fine wires and pulleys at its tip, demonstrating the mechanism by which the discs control the instrument's movements. The surgeon manipulates the disc from the top, causing visible translation of movement within the instrument's housing.

The robotic instruments are connected to the adapter plate on the patient cart, receiving signals from the master console via fiber optic cables. The instruments demonstrate movement with seven degrees of freedom, rotating and moving with precision greater than the human hand. Each instrument has a predefined grasp strength, limiting its pinching force according to system specifications.

The video shows a comparison between a ball grasper and a needle holder, highlighting their predefined grasp strengths. The ball grasper is designed to avoid damaging delicate tissues like the intestine, while the needle holder applies a stronger pinch. The forged bipolar instrument is also visible, emphasizing the importance of understanding the energy and grasp strengths of each tool.

The video displays an 8mm false bipolar robotic instrument, showcasing its bipolar energy capabilities and strong grasp. Additionally, monopolar curved scissors are shown, which are commonly used in hernia surgeries, featuring monopolar energy and an 8mm size.

The video shows curved scissors with seven degrees of freedom, demonstrating their versatility in robotic surgery. Eight millimeter cannulas are used for port placement, with visible ports in place. The remote center, marked by a thick black line, remains fixed in space at the level of the fissure, minimizing trauma to the fissure and abdominal wall, unlike in laparoscopy where the point acts as a fulcrum.

The video shows a detachable cannula seal, highlighting its disposable and reusable components. The cannula seal, ranging from five to eight millimeters, is equipped with an air egress and ingress facility, allowing for gas attachment.

The video shows a robotic surgical system with options for using five or eight millimeter instruments. An optical obturator is demonstrated, which is used for inserting the trocar into the surgical field.

The model for pelvic surgery is shown, with the selection of pelvic anatomy and the patient's left side for the patient cart approach. The robotic system's boom moves into position as the deploy button is pressed for docking.

The robotic system's X-ray boom is fully extended with all four arms positioned based on the pelvic anatomy. The laser lines are directed towards the endoscope port, and the robot is oriented to operate towards itself, demonstrating the boom's rotation and alignment.

The robotic system's boom rotates, folding all its arms back to their original position, while laser lights guide the alignment towards the endoscope. The patient cart is pushed and stored, with Mr. Roshan Kumar pressing the store button.

The robotic boom returns to its original position after completing five movements. The anatomy selection process is demonstrated, with Mr. Roshan selecting the pelvic anatomy and the patient's left side for the procedure. The patient cart is positioned on the patient's left, ready for a pelvic procedure, and Mr. Roshan presses the deploy button for draping.
The robotic system rotates and positions itself for a pelvic procedure, with its arms opening up. The patient card indicates to drive the laser light towards the endoscope port.
Dr. Tushar plugs the endoscope into the vision card, connecting the instrument trolley to the surgical system.
The endoscope is plugged into the vision card, and the patient card is pushed to align the green laser light with the endoscope port for targeting.
The laser light is positioned precisely at the endoscope location, allowing the endoscope to be inserted. Any of the robotic arms can be used to hold the camera for the procedure.
Dr. Pishad uses arm two and arm three to dock the camera port, demonstrating the docking mechanism by pressing a button on the robotic arm.
The endoscope is installed for targeting, with the cable secured and the illuminator switched on. The scope is pointed at the target anatomy, and the targeting button is pressed and held.

The surgeon pushes the instrument clutch and points the endoscope towards the target anatomy. The targeting button is pressed, causing the robotic arms to automatically adjust and rotate to align with the target. After targeting is complete, the remaining robotic arms are docked.
Dr. Tushar docks arm number three while another surgeon docks arm number two, demonstrating the simplified docking process of the surgical system.

The robotic instruments are placed in position on the various arms, completing the docking process. The middle driver is positioned for the right instrument, identified by the machine, while the left hand holds a Maryland bipolar. The instruments are brought under vision using the instrument clutch.

The American Grasper and needle holder are visible in the surgical field as Dr. Tushar positions them. The instruments are now in view, and the surgeon proceeds to the master console to control the robotic system.

The surgeon uses foot pedals to control the four robotic arms, pressing the camera pad with the left foot to zoom in and out, allowing for precise camera movement and close-up views of the surgical field.

The surgeon adjusts the camera view by zooming in and out using the camera paddle, positioning the camera at the desired distance. Instruments with seven degrees of freedom are manipulated, enhancing the view both at the console and on the screen.

The surgeon undocks the instruments from the robotic arms, pressing release levers on the housing to remove the bipolar instrument and camera. The illuminator is switched off, and the arms are undocked using a clip.
The groove rotates, positioning the instrument arms as the entire robotic system gradually slows down.
